inputs.story: A group of long-time skydivers are planning a new jump today. Match each jumper to their suit color, and determine what year each began skydiving and how many total jumps each has under his belt. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
inputs.constraints:
1. Jorge is either the skydiver who wears the red suit or the jumper who started in 1982.
2. The skydiver who wears the pink suit started skydiving 1 year before Otis.
3. The jumper who wears the yellow suit started skydiving sometime after Philip.
4. The jumper who wears the lime green suit started skydiving sometime after the jumper who wears the yellow suit.
5. The jumper who wears the pink suit started skydiving 2 years before Shaun.
inputs.constants:
names: Jorge; Otis; Philip; Shaun.
years: 1982; 1983; 1984; 1985.
suit colors: lime green; pink; red; yellow.
inputs.solution:
shaun	1984	lime green
philip	1982	pink
jorge	1985	red
otis	1983	yellow

constants_formatting.prompt:
Given categorized constants of the form "category: constant_1; constant_2; ...; constant_n", format the category and constants such that:
each category consists of only lowercase letters and underscores, and
each constant is either an integer or a string surrounded by double quotes, e.g., "United States".

There are two ways below to format constants and we must use the same way for all constants of the same category.
1. Turn all constants of the same category into integers with no space or special character.
2. Add double quotes around all constants of the same category.
Note that the 1st way has a higher priority, meaning that we must turn all constants of the same category into integers whenever possible. For example, twice or second can be turned into 2, September can be turned into 9, September 5th can be turned into 5 if all dates are in September, but 9:30am can only be turned into "9:30am" since no integer can represent 9:30am.

Original constants:
Employees: Bonita; Yvette; Tabitha.
Prices: $225; $275; $325.
Wood typse: ash; poplar; sandalwood.

Formatted constants:
employee: "Bonita"; "Yvette"; "Tabitha".
price: 225; 275; 325.
wood_type: "ash"; "poplar"; "sandalwood".

Original constants:
months: January; April; October; December.
times: 8:30AM; 10:30AM; 2:30PM; 3:30PM.
durations: 1 day; 3 days; 11 days; 12 days.

Formatted constants:
months: 1; 4; 10; 12.
time: "8:30AM"; "10:30PM"; "2:30PM"; "3:30PM".
duration: 1; 3; 11; 12.

Original constants:
names: Jorge; Otis; Philip; Shaun.
years: 1982; 1983; 1984; 1985.
suit colors: lime green; pink; red; yellow.

predicates.prompt:
Given a problem and some categorized constants of the form "category: constant_1; constant_2; ...; constant_n", generate the minimum number of predicates to define the relations among the categories of constants. Each generated predicate is of the form "predicate(X1, X2, ..., Xn)" where X1, X2, ..., Xn are different variables and each variable X belongs to one of the categories. For each category, there must exist at least one variable of some predicate that belongs to this category.

Problem 1:
Consider N-Queens Puzzle on a chessboard of size 8x8. The goal is to assign 8 queens on the chessboard so that no two queens can share the same row, column, or diagonal.

Constants:
index_of_row: 1; 2; 3; 4; 5; 6; 7; 8.
index_of_column: 1; 2; 3; 4; 5; 6; 7; 8.

Predicates:
% The categories in Constants include index_of_row and index_of_column. We use different variables Ir and Ic to represent index_of_row and index_of_column.
% We assign a queen at row Ir and column Ic, where Ir belongs to index_of_row and Ic belongs to index_of_column.
assign(Ir, Ic)

Problem 2:
"Against the Grain" offers hand-made wooden furniture at reasonable prices. Each item is made by an in-house employee. Using only the clues that follow, match each item to the employee who crafted it, and determine its price and the type of wood used to make it. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.

Constants:
employee: "Bonita"; "Yvette"; "Tabitha".
price: 225; 275; 325.
wood_type: "ash"; "poplar"; "sandalwood".

Predicates:
% The categories in Constants include employee, price, and wood_type. We use different variables E, P, and W to represent employee, price, and wood_type.
% We match an employee E with price P and wood type W, where E belongs to employee, P belongs to price, and W belongs to wood_type.
match(E, P, W)

Problem 3:
A group of long-time skydivers are planning a new jump today. Match each jumper to their suit color, and determine what year each began skydiving and how many total jumps each has under his belt. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. Jorge is either the skydiver who wears the red suit or the jumper who started in 1982.
2. The skydiver who wears the pink suit started skydiving 1 year before Otis.
3. The jumper who wears the yellow suit started skydiving sometime after Philip.
4. The jumper who wears the lime green suit started skydiving sometime after the jumper who wears the yellow suit.
5. The jumper who wears the pink suit started skydiving 2 years before Shaun.

Constants:
names: "Jorge"; "Otis"; "Philip"; "Shaun".
years: 1982; 1983; 1984; 1985.
suit_colors: "lime green"; "pink"; "red"; "yellow".

Predicates:
predicates.thinking:
Okay, let's tackle Problem 3. The goal is to generate the minimum number of predicates that define the relations among the categories of constants. The constants given are:

- names: "Jorge"; "Otis"; "Philip"; "Shaun".
- years: 1982; 1983; 1984; 1985.
- suit_colors: "lime green"; "pink"; "red"; "yellow".

The problem mentions matching each jumper to their suit color, year they started skydiving, and total jumps. Wait, but the constants don't include "total jumps" as a category. Wait, looking back at the problem statement: "match each jumper to their suit color, and determine what year each began skydiving and how many total jumps each has under his belt." But the constants provided only list names, years, and suit_colors. The "total jumps" isn't listed in the constants. Hmm, maybe that's a mistake, but the problem says "the categories of constants" so we should only use the given constants. So the categories are names, years, suit_colors. Each predicate must relate variables from these categories.

The problem says "each variable X belongs to one of the categories." So we need to create predicates that connect these categories. The key is to have each category covered by at least one variable in some predicate.

Looking at the examples in Problems 1 and 2: Problem 1 has a single predicate "assign(Ir, Ic)" which relates index_of_row and index_of_column. Problem 2 has "match(E, P, W)" relating employee, price, and wood_type. So for Problem 3, we need a predicate that relates the three categories: names, years, suit_colors.

Wait, but the problem says "generate the minimum number of predicates". So maybe we can have one predicate that covers all three categories. Let's check the clues to see if that's possible.

Clues:

1. Jorge is either the skydiver who wears the red suit or the jumper who started in 1982.
   - So Jorge's suit is red OR Jorge's year is 1982.

2. The skydiver who wears the pink suit started skydiving 1 year before Otis.
   - So if someone has suit_color=pink, then their year = Otis's year - 1.

3. The jumper who wears the yellow suit started skydiving sometime after Philip.
   - Yellow suit wearer's year > Philip's year.

4. The jumper who wears the lime green suit started skydiving sometime after the jumper who wears the yellow suit.
   - Lime green year > yellow year.

5. The jumper who wears the pink suit started skydiving 2 years before Shaun.
   - Pink suit year = Shaun's year - 2.

So, to model these, we need a predicate that connects names, years, and suit_colors. For example, a predicate like "jumper(Name, Year, Color)" where Name is from names, Year from years, Color from suit_colors. Then all the clues can be expressed using this predicate.

Let's see if one predicate is sufficient. Each clue refers to a relation between two or three of these categories. For example, clue 2: "pink suit started 1 year before Otis" would translate to: if jumper(Otis, Y, _) and jumper(_, Y-1, "pink"), then... Wait, but the predicate would need to be used to define the relations. So the predicate "jumper(Name, Year, Color)" would allow us to express that for a given Name, Year, Color. Then the clues can be written as constraints on this predicate.

The problem states: "generate the minimum number of predicates to define the relations among the categories of constants." Each predicate is a relation between variables from different categories. Since there are three categories, the minimal number would be one predicate that relates all three. Because if we had separate predicates for each pair, that would be more, but the minimal should be one.

Wait, in Problem 2, they used a single predicate "match(E, P, W)" covering all three categories. Similarly, Problem 1 uses "assign(Ir, Ic)" for two categories. So for three categories, one predicate is sufficient.

The problem says "each variable X belongs to one of the categories" and "for each category, there must exist at least one variable of some predicate that belongs to this category." So in a single predicate with three variables (one for each category), all categories are covered.

Therefore, the minimal predicate should be "jumper(Name, Year, Color)", where Name is from names, Year from years, Color from suit_colors.

Let me check if all clues can be expressed with this. For example, clue 1: Jorge is either the red suit wearer or started in 1982. So in terms of the predicate: jumper("Jorge", _, "red") OR jumper("Jorge", 1982, _).

Clue 2: pink suit started 1 year before Otis. So jumper(Otis, Y, _) and jumper(_, Y-1, "pink").

Similarly, clue 5: pink suit started 2 years before Shaun. So jumper("Shaun", Y, _) and jumper(_, Y-2, "pink").

So yes, all clues can be expressed using the single predicate. Therefore, the minimal number is 1, and the predicate should be "jumper(Name, Year, Color)".

Wait, but the problem says "generate the minimum number of predicates", so if one is enough, then we just need that one.

Check the constants: names, years, suit_colors. The predicate must have variables from each category. So the predicate should have one variable for each category. So "jumper(Name, Year, Color)" where Name is names, Year is years, Color is suit_colors.

Yes, that covers all three categories. So the answer should be one predicate: "jumper(Name, Year, Color)".

Wait, but the problem says "each variable X belongs to one of the categories". So in the predicate, each argument is a variable from a category. So "jumper(Name, Year, Color)" is correct, with Name ∈ names, Year ∈ years, Color ∈ suit_colors.

No other predicates are needed because all categories are covered by this one predicate. So the minimal number is 1.
predicates.response:
The problem requires defining the minimum number of predicates to capture the relationships among the categories: `names`, `years`, and `suit_colors`. Each predicate must relate variables from different categories, and all categories must be covered by at least one variable in the predicates.

### Analysis:
- **Categories**: `names` (4 options), `years` (4 options), `suit_colors` (4 options).
- **Clues**: All clues involve relationships between **names**, **years**, and **suit_colors** (e.g., "Jorge wears red" → relates `names` and `suit_colors`; "pink suit started 1 year before Otis" → relates `suit_colors` and `years`).
- **Minimal Predicate**: A single predicate that combines all three categories is sufficient. This avoids redundancy and satisfies the requirement of covering all categories with the fewest predicates.

### Solution:
The predicate **`jumper(Name, Year, Color)`** is the minimal and sufficient relation:
- `Name` ∈ `names`
- `Year` ∈ `years`
- `Color` ∈ `suit_colors`

This predicate directly models the assignment of a **name**, **year**, and **suit color** to each skydiver, which aligns with all clues (e.g., "Jorge is the red suit wearer" → `jumper("Jorge", _, "red")`).

### Final Answer:
```
jumper(Name, Year, Color)
```

search_space.prompt:
Given some categorized constants in the form "category: constant_1; constant_2; ...; constant_n" and some predicates about the relation among different categories of constants, write ASP (Answer Set Programming) rules to generate the search space of possible relations. In your response, include a SINGLE ASP code-block with the rules - it will be automatically parsed from your response.

Constants:
employee: "Bonita"; "Yvette"; "Tabitha".
price: 225; 275; 325.
wood_type: "ash"; "poplar"; "sandalwood".

Predicates:
% The categories include employee, price, and wood_type. We use different variables E, P, and W to represent employee, price, and wood_type.
% We match an employee E with price P and wood type W, where E belongs to employee, P belongs to price, and W belongs to wood_type.
match(E, P, W)

ASP Rules:
```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
employee("Bonita"; "Yvette"; "Tabitha").
price(225; 275; 325).
wood_type("ash"; "poplar"; "sandalwood").

% For each employee E, it matches with exactly 1 price P and 1 wood type W.
{match(E, P, W): price(P), wood_type(W)}=1 :- employee(E).
```

Constants:
names: "Jorge"; "Otis"; "Philip"; "Shaun".
years: 1982; 1983; 1984; 1985.
suit_colors: "lime green"; "pink"; "red"; "yellow".

Predicates:
jumper(Name, Year, Color)

ASP Rules:

The rules defined the constants and then used a cardinality constraint to ensure each employee matches exactly one price and one wood type.

Now, for the new problem, the constants are names, years, suit_colors, and the predicate is jumper(Name, Year, Color). So I need to follow the same pattern.

First, I need to define the constants for each category. The categories here are names, years, suit_colors. So I should write:

years(1982; 1983; 1984; 1985).
suit_colors("lime green"; "pink"; "red"; "yellow").

Wait, but in the previous example, they used the category names as the predicate names. Like employee/1, price/1, wood_type/1. So here, it should be names/1, years/1, suit_colors/1.

Then, the predicate is jumper(Name, Year, Color), which matches a name, year, and color. The rule should say that for each name, there's exactly one year and one color. So similar to the previous rule, but using the new categories.

The previous rule was: {match(E, P, W): price(P), wood_type(W)}=1 :- employee(E).

So translating that, it's a choice rule where for each employee, exactly one combination of price and wood_type is chosen. So for the new case, it should be:

{ jumper(Name, Year, Color) : years(Year), suit_colors(Color) } = 1 :- names(Name).

Wait, but the predicate is jumper(Name, Year, Color), so the variables are Name, Year, Color. So the rule should be that for each Name (from names), there's exactly one Year (from years) and one Color (from suit_colors).

So the rule would be:

{ jumper(Name, Year, Color) : years(Year), suit_colors(Color) } = 1 :- names(Name).

Yes, that's the same structure as the example. The previous example used match(E, P, W), here it's jumper(Name, Year, Color).

Let me check if the constants are correctly defined. The constants given are:

names: "Jorge"; "Otis"; "Philip"; "Shaun".
years: 1982; 1983; 1984; 1985.
suit_colors: "lime green"; "pink"; "red"; "yellow".

So in ASP, the constants are defined as facts. So:

names("Jorge"; "Otis"; "Philip"; "Shaun").
years(1982; 1983; 1984; 1985).
suit_colors("lime green"; "pink"; "red"; "yellow").

Then the rule for the jumper predicate. The user's example used "match(E, P, W)" with the variables, so here it's "jumper(Name, Year, Color)".

So the rule should be:

{ jumper(Name, Year, Color) : years(Year), suit_colors(Color) } = 1 :- names(Name).

That's the key part. Let me confirm: for each name, exactly one year and one color. The cardinality constraint { ... }=1 ensures that exactly one of the possible combinations (Year, Color) is selected for each Name.

Wait, in the example, the rule was {match(E, P, W): price(P), wood_type(W)}=1 :- employee(E). So for each employee, exactly one (P, W) pair. Similarly here, for each name, exactly one (Year, Color) pair.

Yes, that's correct. So the ASP code should have the three category definitions and the cardinality rule.

Wait, the user's previous example had the constants as employee/1, price/1, wood_type/1. So here, the constants are names/1, years/1, suit_colors/1.

So the code should be:

years(1982; 1983; 1984; 1985).
suit_colors("lime green"; "pink"; "red"; "yellow").

{ jumper(Name, Year, Color) : years(Year), suit_colors(Color) } = 1 :- names(Name).

That's all. Let me check if there's any other predicates or rules needed. The problem statement says to generate the search space of possible relations, so the only rule needed is the cardinality constraint to ensure each name is matched to exactly one year and color.

I think that's it. Let me make sure the syntax is correct. In ASP, the choice rule uses { ... } = N, and the variables are defined with the categories. The variables Year and Color are constrained by the years and suit_colors predicates.

Yes, the rule is correct. So the final ASP code block should have those three facts and the one rule.
search_space.response:
```asp
names("Jorge"; "Otis"; "Philip"; "Shaun").
years(1982; 1983; 1984; 1985).
suit_colors("lime green"; "pink"; "red"; "yellow").

{ jumper(Name, Year, Color) : years(Year), suit_colors(Color) } = 1 :- names(Name).
```

paraphrasing.prompt:
Copy a sequence of numbered sentences.

If a sentence is of the form "N. Of A and B, one is C and the other is D", replace it with 3 sentences below.
N.1 A and B are different.
N.2 A is C or D.
N.3 B is C or D.

For every sentence, if it is not of the form "N. Of ... and ...", simply copy it without replacement. An easy way to determine if a sentence is not of the above form is to check if its first word is not of.

In the following example, one sentence is of the above form.
Given:
1. The squad from Grenada ended with 2 silver medals.
2. Of the team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals, one finished with 2 gold medals and the other finished with 1 gold medal.
Copy:
1. The squad from Grenada ended with 2 silver medals.
2.1 The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
2.2 The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
2.3 The team that won 10 silver medals finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.

In the following example, no sentence is of the above form.
Given:
1. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
2. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.
Copy:
1. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
2. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.

Given:
1. Jorge is either the skydiver who wears the red suit or the jumper who started in 1982.
2. The skydiver who wears the pink suit started skydiving 1 year before Otis.
3. The jumper who wears the yellow suit started skydiving sometime after Philip.
4. The jumper who wears the lime green suit started skydiving sometime after the jumper who wears the yellow suit.
5. The jumper who wears the pink suit started skydiving 2 years before Shaun.
Copy:

- Starts with a number followed by a period
- Contains "Of [A] and [B], one is [C] and the other is [D]"

Looking at the given sentences:

1. "Jorge is either the skydiver who wears the red suit or the jumper who started in 1982." 
   - This doesn't have "Of A and B, one is C and the other is D" - it uses "either...or" but lacks the "Of" structure. So it stays as is.

2. "The skydiver who wears the pink suit started skydiving 1 year before Otis."
   - No "Of" structure, so unchanged.

3. "The jumper who wears the yellow suit started skydiving sometime after Philip."
   - Same as above.

4. "The jumper who wears the lime green suit started skydiving sometime after the jumper who wears the yellow suit."
   - Still no "Of" pattern.

5. "The jumper who wears the pink suit started skydiving 2 years before Shaun."
   - No match.

Wait, let me double-check sentence 1 again. The pattern requires "Of A and B, one is C and the other is D". Sentence 1 says "Jorge is either the skydiver... or the jumper...", which is similar but doesn't have the "Of" part. The example given in the problem shows that sentences must start with "Of" to qualify. The user explicitly says: "An easy way to determine if a sentence is not of the above form is to check if its first word is not of." 

So for sentence 1, the first word is "Jorge", not "Of", so it's safe to leave it. None of the sentences start with "Of", so none need replacement. 

Confirming with the second example in the problem: 
- "The $275 item is either..." was copied as-is because it doesn't start with "Of". 

Therefore, all five sentences should be copied directly without changes.

prompt.content: You are a semantic parser to turn clues in a problem into logical rules using only provided constants and predicates.

Consider the constraint in the following form
<C1>; <C2>; ...; <Cm> :- <L1>, <L2>, ..., <Ln>.
which says that if the conjunction "<L1> and <L2> and ... and <Ln>" is true, then the disjunction of comparisons "<C1> or <C2> or ... or <Cm>" must be true.

One can also add a restriction that "exactly k of <C1>, <C2>, ..., <Cm> is true" by using the following form
{<C1>; <C2>; ...; <Cm>}=k :- <L1>, <L2>, ..., <Ln>.

Given a problem, extract all constraints from the clues in the problem using only the provided constants and predicates. In your response, include a SINGLE ASP code-block with the constraints - it will be automatically parsed from your response.
Ok. I will only write constraints under the provided forms.

Problem 1:
"Against the Grain" offers hand-made wooden furniture at reasonable prices. Each item is made by an in-house employee. Using only the clues that follow, match each item to the employee who crafted it, and determine its price and the type of wood used to make it. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.

Constants:
employee: "Bonita"; "Yvette"; "Tabitha".
price: 225; 275; 325.
wood_type: "ash"; "poplar"; "sandalwood".

Predicates:
% The categories include employee, price, and wood_type. We use different variables E, P, and W to represent employee, price, and wood_type.
% We match an employee E with price P and wood type W, where E belongs to employee, P belongs to price, and W belongs to wood_type.
match(E, P, W)

Constraints:

```asp
% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{E1=E2; P1=P2; W1=W2}=0 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), (E1,P1,W1)!=(E2,P2,W2).

% 1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
P=325 :- match(E,P,W), E="Bonita".

% 2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
P1>P2 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), W1="poplar", E2="Yvette".

% 3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
P1=P2-50 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), E1="Tabitha", W2="sandalwood".

% 4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.
{W="ash"; E="Yvette"}=1 :- match(E,P,W), P=275.
```

Problem 2:
The Winter Olympics have just wrapped up in Norway. Using only the clues that follow, determine the number of gold, silver and bronze medals won by each country. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. The four teams are the team from Bolivia, the team that won 3 gold medals, the team that won 6 silver medals, and the team from Argentina.
2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
5. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams.
6. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
8. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.

Constants:
country: "Argentina"; "Bolivia"; "Grenada"; "Oman".
silver_medals: 2; 6; 10; 11.
gold_medals: 1; 2; 3; 4.

Predicates:
% The categories include country, silver_medals, and gold_medals. We use different variables C, S, and G to represent country, silver_medals, and gold_medals.
% We assign a country C with silver medals S and gold medals G, where C belongs to country, S belongs to silver_medals, and G belongs to gold_medals.
assign(C, S, G)

Constraints:

```asp
% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{C1=C2; S1=S2; G1=G2}=0 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), (C1,S1,G1)!=(C2,S2,G2).

% 1. The four teams are the team from Bolivia, the team that won 3 gold medals, the team that won 6 silver medals, and the team from Argentina.
{C="Bolivia"; G=3; S=6; C="Argentina"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G).

% 2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Oman", S2=10.

% 3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
{G=2; G=1}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), C="Oman".

% 5. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C2!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C1!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".

% 6. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
{C="Argentina"; S=2}=0 :- assign(C,S,G), G=4.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Argentina", S2=2.

% 8. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.
{S=6; C="Grenada"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), G=2.
```

Problem 3:
A group of long-time skydivers are planning a new jump today. Match each jumper to their suit color, and determine what year each began skydiving and how many total jumps each has under his belt. 3. The jumper who wears the yellow suit started skydiving sometime after Philip.
4. The jumper who wears the lime green suit started skydiving sometime after the jumper who wears the yellow suit.
5. The jumper who wears the pink suit started skydiving 2 years before Shaun.

Constants:
names: "Jorge"; "Otis"; "Philip"; "Shaun".
years: 1982; 1983; 1984; 1985.
suit_colors: "lime green"; "pink"; "red"; "yellow".

Predicates:
jumper(Name, Year, Color)

initial_run.asp_program:
names("Jorge"; "Otis"; "Philip"; "Shaun").
years(1982; 1983; 1984; 1985).
suit_colors("lime green"; "pink"; "red"; "yellow").

{ jumper(Name, Year, Color) : years(Year), suit_colors(Color) } = 1 :- names(Name).

% Uniqueness constraint
{C1=C2; Y1=Y2; Col1=Col2}=0 :- jumper(C1,Y1,Col1), jumper(C2,Y2,Col2), (C1,Y1,Col1)!=(C2,Y2,Col2).

% Clue 1: Jorge is either the red suit or started in 1982
{Col="red"; Y=1982}=1 :- jumper("Jorge", Y, Col).

% Clue 2: Pink suit started 1 year before Otis
Y2 = Y1 + 1 :- jumper(_, Y1, "pink"), jumper("Otis", Y2, _).

% Clue 3: Yellow suit started after Philip
Y3 > Y4 :- jumper(_, Y3, "yellow"), jumper("Philip", Y4, _).

% Clue 4: Lime green suit started after yellow suit
Y5 > Y6 :- jumper(_, Y5, "lime green"), jumper(_, Y6, "yellow").

% Clue 5: Pink suit started 2 years before Shaun
Y7 = Y8 + 2 :- jumper(_, Y8, "pink"), jumper("Shaun", Y7, _).
